column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=EXCELLENT CLAM SOUP.
#Text=--Take forty or fifty clams, and wash and scrub the\r
#Text=outsides of the shells till they are perfectly clean.
#Text=Then put them into\r
#Text=a pot with just sufficient water to keep them from burning.
#Text=The water\r
#Text=must boil hard when you put in the clams.
#Text=In about a quarter of an hour\r
#Text=the shells will open, and the liquor run out and mix with the water,\r
#Text=which must be saved for the soup, and strained into a soup-pot, after\r
#Text=the clams are taken out.
#Text=Extract the clams from their shells, and cut\r
#Text=them up small.
#Text=Then put them into the soup-pot, adding a minced onion, a\r
#Text=saucer of finely chopped celery, or a table-spoonful of celery seed, and\r
#Text=a dozen blades of mace, with a dozen whole pepper-corns.
#Text=No salt, as the\r
#Text=clam-liquor will be quite salt enough.
#Text=If the liquid is not in\r
#Text=sufficient quantity to fill a large tureen, add some milk.
#Text=Thicken the\r
#Text=soup with two large table-spoonfuls of fresh butter rolled in flour.
#Text=Let\r
#Text=it boil a quarter of an hour or twenty minutes.
#Text=Just before you take it\r
#Text=from the fire, stir in, gradually, the beaten yolks of five eggs; and\r
#Text=then take up the soup, and pour it into a tureen, the bottom of which\r
#Text=is covered with toasted bread, cut into square dice about an inch in\r
#Text=size.
#Text=VEAL RISSOLES.
#Text=--Take as much fine wheat bread as will weigh one pound,\r
#Text=after all the crust is cut off.
#Text=Slice it; put it into a pan and pour\r
#Text=over it as much rich milk as will soak it thoroughly.
#Text=After it has\r
#Text=soaked a quarter of an hour, lay it in a sieve and press it dry.
#Text=Mince\r
#Text=as finely as possible a pound of veal cutlet with six ounces of veal\r
#Text=suet; then mix in gradually the bread; adding a salt-spoonful of salt, a\r
#Text=slight sprinkling of cayenne, and a small tea-spoonful of powdered mace\r
#Text=and nutmeg mixed; also the yellow rind of a lemon grated.
#Text=Beat two eggs,\r
#Text=and moisten the mixture with them.
#Text=Then divide it into equal portions,\r
#Text=and with a little flour on your hands roll it into oval balls rather\r
#Text=smaller than an egg.
#Text=Strew over them some dry bread-crumbs; then fry\r
#Text=them in lard or fresh butter--drain them well, and send them to table\r
#Text=hot.
#Text=For gravy (which should be commenced before the rissoles) put some\r
#Text=bits and trimmings of veal into a small sauce-pan, with as much water as\r
#Text=will cover them; a very little pepper and salt; and three or four blades\r
#Text=of mace.
#Text=Cover the sauce-pan closely, and let the meat stew till all the\r
#Text=strength is extracted; skimming it well.
#Text=Then strain it; return the\r
#Text=liquid to the sauce-pan; add a bit of butter rolled in flour; and\r
#Text=squeeze in the juice of a lemon.
#Text=Give it a boil up; and then, at the\r
#Text=last, stir in the beaten yolk of an egg.
#Text=Serve up this gravy in a\r
#Text=sauce-boat, to eat with the rissoles.
#Text=A NICE WAY OF COOKING GAME.
#Text=--Pheasants, partridges, quails, grouse,\r
#Text=plovers are excellent stuffed with chesnuts: boiled, peeled, and\r
#Text=mashed or pounded.
#Text=Cover the birds with very thin slices of cold ham;\r
#Text=then enclose them in vine-leaves tied on securely so as to keep in the\r
#Text=gravy.
#Text=Lay them in a deep dish, and bake them in a close oven that has\r
#Text=nothing else in it, (for instance an iron dutch oven,) that the game may\r
#Text=imbibe no other flavour.
#Text=When done, remove the ham and the vine leaves,\r
#Text=and dish the birds with the gravy that is about them.
#Text=Pheasants are unfit to eat after the first snow, as they then, for want\r
#Text=of other food, are apt to feed on wild laurel berries, which give their\r
#Text=flesh a disagreeably bitter taste, and are said to have sometimes\r
#Text=produced deleterious effects on persons who have eaten it.
#Text=SWEETMEAT DUMPLINGS.
#Text=--Make a paste of half a pound of fresh butter, or\r
#Text=finely minced suet, and a pound of flour, moistened with a very little\r
#Text=cold water.
#Text=Beat the lump of paste on all sides with a rolling-pin.
#Text=Then\r
#Text=roll it out into a sheet, and divide it into equal portions.
#Text=Lay on the\r
#Text=middle of each two halves (laid on each other) of preserved peaches, or\r
#Text=quinces, or large preserved plums.
#Text=Then close the paste round the\r
#Text=sweetmeat, so as to form a dumpling.
#Text=Have ready a pot of boiling water.
#Text=Throw the dumplings into it, tied up in little cloths, and let them boil\r
#Text=twenty-five minutes or half an hour.
#Text=Try one first, to see if they are\r
#Text=done.
#Text=When quite done, take them up, dip them in cold water, turn them\r
#Text=out of the cloths, and send the dumplings to table immediately.
#Text=Eat them\r
#Text=with sugar only, or with sweetened cream.
#Text=These dumplings may be made with jam or marmalade, formed into a heap or\r
#Text=lump, and laid in the centre of each piece of paste.
#Text=You may fry these fritters in lard, but they will not be so nice as if\r
#Text=done in fresh butter.
#Text=COCOA-NUT CREAM may be made as above; substituting for the almonds a\r
#Text=pound of cocoa-nut grated finely.
#Text=When it has boiled, and is taken from\r
#Text=the fire, stir into the cream a wine-glass of rose-water.
#Text=A similar cream may be made with pounded pistachio nuts.
#Text=Pecan nuts, blanched and pounded, (adding occasionally a little cold\r
#Text=water to take off the oiliness,) may be boiled as above, with cream,\r
#Text=sugar, and spice.
#Text=All these creams may be frozen, and served up as ice-cream.
#Text=VANILLA CREAM.
#Text=--Boil a vanilla bean in half a pint of rich milk, till\r
#Text=the milk is highly flavoured with the vanilla.
#Text=Then (having taken out\r
#Text=the bean) strain the milk into a pint of thick cream.
#Text=Beat the yolks of\r
#Text=five eggs till very light, and then mix gradually with the beaten egg a\r
#Text=quarter of a pound of powdered loaf-sugar, beating it in very hard.
#Text=Set\r
#Text=the cream over hot coals, and add to it by degrees the egg and sugar.
#Text=Stir it continually till it is on the point of coming to a boil.
#Text=It must\r
#Text=be very thick and smooth.
#Text=Cover the bottom and sides of a glass bowl or\r
#Text=dish, with three quarters of a pound of lady-cake, cut into nice even\r
#Text=slices.
#Text=Pour on the mixture, and then set the bowl on ice or snow till\r
#Text=wanted.
#Text=THE BEST WAY OF PRESERVING PINE-APPLES.
#Text=--Take six large, fine, ripe\r
#Text=pine-apples.
#Text=Make them very clean, but do not pare off the rind, or cut\r
#Text=off the leaves.
#Text=Put them, whole, into a very large and very clean pot or\r
#Text=kettle.
#Text=Fill it up with cold water, and boil the pine-apples till they\r
#Text=are so tender that you can penetrate them all through with a twig from a\r
#Text=broom.
#Text=Then take them out and drain them.
#Text=When cool enough to handle\r
#Text=without inconvenience, remove the leaves, and pare off the rind.
#Text=The\r
#Text=rind and leaves being left on, while boiling, will keep in the flavour\r
#Text=of the fruit.
#Text=Cut the pine-apples into round slices, about half an inch\r
#Text=thick, extracting the core from the centre, so as to leave a round hole\r
#Text=in the middle of every slice.
#Text=Weigh them; and to each pound allow a\r
#Text=pound of double-refined loaf-sugar, broken up and powdered.
#Text=Cover the\r
#Text=bottom of a large dish, or dishes, with a layer of the sugar.
#Text=On this,\r
#Text=place a layer of pine-apple slices; then a layer of sugar; then one of\r
#Text=pine-apple; and so till the pine-apple slices are all covered; finishing\r
#Text=with a layer of sugar.
#Text=Let them stand twenty-four hours.
#Text=Then drain the\r
#Text=slices from the syrup, and lay them in wide jars.
#Text=Put the syrup into a\r
#Text=clean preserving-kettle, and boil and skim it till the scum ceases to\r
#Text=rise.
#Text=Then pour it hot upon the pine-apple.
#Text=While still warm, cover the\r
#Text=jars closely, and paste paper over them.
#Text=They will be found very fine.
#Text=QUINCES may be preserved in a similar manner; first boiling them whole,\r
#Text=with the skin on; then peeling them, and extracting the cores; then\r
#Text=slicing the quinces into round, thin pieces, and letting them stand\r
#Text=twenty-four hours in layers of sugar.
#Text=Boil the syrup, and pour it over\r
#Text=the quinces, after they are in the jars.
#Text=Save the parings and cores, and also some of the water in which the\r
#Text=quinces were boiled.
#Text=Weigh the boiled cores and parings, and to each\r
#Text=pound allow a half-pint of the quince-water.
#Text=Set them over the fire, in\r
#Text=a clean kettle, and boil them, till dissolved as much as possible.
#Text=Then\r
#Text=strain them through a linen bag.
#Text=To each pint of juice allow a pound of\r
#Text=loaf-sugar, powdered.
#Text=Having washed the kettle, put in the sugar; pour\r
#Text=on it the quince-liquor; and boil it till it becomes a jelly.
#Text=Try it, by\r
#Text=holding a spoonful in the open air, and, if all is right, it will\r
#Text=congeal very soon.
#Text=TO PRESERVE GREEN GAGES.
#Text=--Take gages that are perfectly ripe.
#Text=Weigh\r
#Text=them; and to each pound of fruit allow a pound of the best\r
#Text=double-refined loaf-sugar, broken up.
#Text=Put a layer of grape-leaves in the\r
#Text=bottom and round the sides of your preserving-kettle.
#Text=Then put in the\r
#Text=gages, interspersing them thickly with vine-leaves, and covering them\r
#Text=with a thick layer.
#Text=Pour in just enough of water to keep them from\r
#Text=burning.
#Text=Set the kettle over the fire, cover it, and let it simmer\r
#Text=slowly till the gages are well greened.
#Text=Then take them out, and spread\r
#Text=them on a large dish to cool.
#Text=Afterwards prick them in several places\r
#Text=with a needle.
#Text=Having washed the kettle clean, put the sugar into it\r
#Text=with a very little water,--about half a pint to each pound of sugar.
#Text=Set\r
#Text=it over the fire, and boil and skim it till no more scum rises.
#Text=Then put\r
#Text=in the gages, and boil them half an hour.
#Text=When done, and cold, put them\r
#Text=into glass jars, and pour the syrup over them.
#Text=Paste paper closely down\r
#Text=over the lids of the jars.
#Text=POTATOE ROLLS.
#Text=--Take fine large potatoes.
#Text=Boil, peel, and mash them.
#Text=Then rub the mashed potatoe through a sieve.
#Text=To each potatoe allow a\r
#Text=pint of sifted flour; a table-spoonful of strong fresh yeast; a jill of\r
#Text=milk-warm water; a salt-spoon of salt; the yolk of an egg; and a bit of\r
#Text=fresh butter about the size of a large hickory-nut.
#Text=Mix together in a\r
#Text=large broad pan the flour, the mashed potatoe, and the salt.
#Text=Make a hole\r
#Text=in the centre of the mixture, and pour into it the yeast mixed with the\r
#Text=warm water.
#Text=Sprinkle a little flour over the top, and mix in a little\r
#Text=from round the sides of the hole.
#Text=Cover it with a clean towel, and over\r
#Text=that a flannel, and set it near the fire to rise.
#Text=When the dough is\r
#Text=quite light, and cracked all over the surface, knead in the butter and\r
#Text=also the yolks of eggs, having previously beaten them well, and add a\r
#Text=small tea-spoonful of soda dissolved in a little warm water.
#Text=Then divide\r
#Text=the dough into equal parts, make it into long-shaped rolls, and lay them\r
#Text=in a tin or iron pan sprinkled with flour.
#Text=Cover them, and again set\r
#Text=them to rise in a warm place.
#Text=When perfectly light, (which should be in\r
#Text=about an hour,) set the pan into the oven, and bake the rolls brown.
#Text=They are best when quite fresh.
#Text=Pull them open with your fingers, and\r
#Text=eat them with butter.
#Text=LEMON MACCAROONS.
#Text=--Take four large ripe lemons, and rub off the yellow\r
#Text=surface of the rind, upon a lump of sugar.
#Text=Then powder that sugar, and\r
#Text=add to it not quite a pound of loaf-sugar, already powdered.
#Text=Break four\r
#Text=eggs into a shallow pan, and beat them till very thick and light.
#Text=Then\r
#Text=add the juice of the lemons, squeezed through a strainer, and a\r
#Text=tea-spoonful of powdered nutmeg and cinnamon, and stir in the sugar, a\r
#Text=little at a time, alternately with three large heaped-up table-spoonfuls\r
#Text=of sifted flour.
#Text=A little more flour may, probably, be found necessary.
#Text=Mix the whole, thoroughly, so as to form a soft paste.
#Text=Have ready some\r
#Text=shallow, square baking-pans, or sheets of iron, the bottoms covered with\r
#Text=white paper, laid smoothly in.
#Text=Moisten your hands with water, and then\r
#Text=take up portions of the mixture, and roll them into balls, about the\r
#Text=size of a large plum, laying them, as you proceed, upon the paper, but\r
#Text=rather more than an inch apart.
#Text=Lastly, with the blade of a knife,\r
#Text=dipped in water, smooth the surface of each.
#Text=Set them into a moderate\r
#Text=oven, and bake them brown.
#Text=Try one, when you think they are done.
#Text=If not\r
#Text=sufficiently baked, let them remain longer in the oven.
#Text=As soon as they\r
#Text=are cold, loosen them from the paper, by slipping under them a\r
#Text=broad-bladed knife.
#Text=Orange maccaroons may be made in this manner, using\r
#Text=the grated rind of two oranges only, but the juice of four.
#Text=To make\r
#Text=vanilla maccaroons, boil, in a covered vessel, a vanilla bean, with as\r
#Text=much milk as will barely cover it.
#Text=When the milk is strongly flavoured\r
#Text=with the vanilla, strain it, and, when cold, add it to the beaten egg.
#Text=Then stir in, gradually, the sugar, spice, and flour, and proceed as\r
#Text=above.
#Text=VERY FINE INK.
#Text=--Into a large jar or pitcher put half a pound of the best\r
#Text=Aleppo galls, broken up with a hammer or flat-iron; but not pounded.
#Text=Pour on two quarts of soft water, nearly of boiling heat.
#Text=Cover the\r
#Text=vessel; and let it stand on a warm hearth or in the hot sun for a\r
#Text=fortnight; stirring it to the bottom twice a day, with a stick.
#Text=At the\r
#Text=end of the fortnight, add two ounces of green copperas; two ounces of\r
#Text=logwood chips; two ounces of gum-arabic; half an ounce of alum; and half\r
#Text=an ounce of sugar-candy.
#Text=Let the whole remain in a moderate heat a\r
#Text=fortnight longer; stirring it twice a day.
#Text=Keep the mouth of the vessel\r
#Text=covered with paper only, tied down over it.
#Text=On the last day, do not stir\r
#Text=it, but pour the ink through a strainer into another vessel, and then\r
#Text=with a funnel transfer it to bottles.
#Text=Pour a small tea-spoonful or more\r
#Text=of brandy into the top of each bottle, if small.
#Text=To a pint bottle there\r
#Text=should be a table-spoonful of brandy.
#Text=This will preserve the ink from\r
#Text=moulding.
#Text=Cork the bottles well, and seal the corks.
#Text=Keep them in a\r
#Text=place of temperate heat.
#Text=In buying Aleppo galls get those that are dark coloured, heavy, and free\r
#Text=from holes.
#Text=TO DESTROY FLIES.
#Text=--Get, at a druggist’s, some Egyptian or Fly-killing\r
#Text=paper.
#Text=Lay a piece of it on an old plate, and keep it moist by wetting\r
#Text=it frequently with water.
#Text=It will soon be found covered with dead flies.
#Text=Shake them off, and wet the paper again.
#Text=Or mix together a table-spoonful of powdered black pepper, the same\r
#Text=quantity of brown sugar, and as much milk as will make it into a thin\r
#Text=paste.
#Text=Set it about on saucers.
#Text=It will attract the flies, and they will\r
#Text=die on eating it.
#Text=TO DESTROY GARDEN ANTS.
#Text=--Mix together half a pound of flour of\r
#Text=brimstone, and four ounces of potash.
#Text=Put them into an iron pot or pan,\r
#Text=and stir it over the fire till they are dissolved, and well\r
#Text=incorporated.
#Text=Then pound them to a powder.
#Text=Put the powder into a glass\r
#Text=jar, with a cover, and keep it for use.
#Text=Infuse some of this powder in a\r
#Text=cup of water, and sprinkle with it the places that are infested by ants.
#Text=They will soon disappear.
#Text=TO EXPEL SMALL ANTS.
#Text=--Mix a tea-spoonful of tartar emetic in two\r
#Text=table-spoonfuls of molasses.
#Text=Stir this into a small saucer of water, and\r
#Text=set it where you have seen the ants.
#Text=Let it remain all night; and in the\r
#Text=morning you will find a great number of ants lying dead on the surface\r
#Text=of the water, and the others will have been frightened away.
#Text=Skim off\r
#Text=the dead ants, and set the saucer in any other place where these insects\r
#Text=have appeared.
#Text=This we know, by experience, to be an excellent remedy\r
#Text=for the little ants with which so many houses are infested, and which\r
#Text=swarm over sweet things.
#Text=MICE.
#Text=--An excellent preparation for expelling mice and rats is Levy’s\r
#Text=Exterminator, spread upon bread or cheese, and laid about the places\r
#Text=they frequent.
#Text=It is a preparation of phosphorus; and after one mouse\r
#Text=has eaten it and (of course) died, the others will disappear.
#Text=It is to\r
#Text=be had of most druggists; and will also destroy cockroaches, by\r
#Text=spreading it on bits of cake or something similar, and laying it at\r
#Text=night on the kitchen hearth, and in the closets.
#Text=We highly recommend it.
#Text=If you propose to destroy a mouse by arsenic spread on bread and butter,\r
#Text=sprinkle on the arsenic a drop or two of oil of rhodium, and the mouse\r
#Text=will unfailingly be attracted to the poison.
#Text=Place beside it a saucer of\r
#Text=water, and as soon as he has eaten of the poisoned bread and butter, he\r
#Text=will drink, and then die on the spot.
#Text=Oil of aniseed, spread on the bait, will attract them into a trap.
#Text=TO DESTROY CATERPILLARS.
#Text=--Mix together twelve ounces of powdered\r
#Text=quick-lime, two ounces of snuff, two ounces of fine salt, and two ounces\r
#Text=of powdered sulphur.
#Text=Strew this mixture over the caterpillars, or\r
#Text=dissolve it in five gallons of water; keep it in a convenient vessel,\r
#Text=and sprinkle with it places where they abound.
#Text=Any garden insects may be destroyed in this manner.
#Text=TO DESTROY WORMS IN GARDEN WALKS.
#Text=--Pour into the worm-holes a strong\r
#Text=lye, made of wood-ashes, lime, and water.
#Text=Or, if more convenient, use,\r
#Text=for this purpose, strong salt and water.
#Text=TO DESTROY THE BEE-MILLER.
#Text=--This insect, whose night-visits are so\r
#Text=destructive to bees, may be destroyed by mixing a large wine-glass of\r
#Text=vinegar with a pint of water, that has been made very sweet with honey.
#Text=Set it in a bowl on the top of the hive, or beside it.
#Text=It will attract\r
#Text=the miller, and then drown him.
#Text=CURE FOR PRICKLY HEAT.
#Text=--Mix a large portion of wheat bran with either\r
#Text=cold or lukewarm water, and use it as a bath twice or thrice a day.
#Text=Children who are covered with prickly heat in warm weather will be thus\r
#Text=effectually relieved from that tormenting eruption.
#Text=As soon as it begins\r
#Text=to appear on the neck, face, or arms, commence using the bran-water on\r
#Text=these parts repeatedly through the day, and it may probably spread no\r
#Text=farther.
#Text=If it does, the bran-water bath will certainly cure it, if\r
#Text=persisted in.
#Text=BROWN MIXTURE FOR A COUGH.
#Text=--Mix in a large bottle, half an ounce of\r
#Text=liquorice; a quarter of an ounce of gum-arabic; two tea-spoonfuls of\r
#Text=antimonial wine; sixty drops of laudanum; and half a pint of water.
#Text=Shake it well, and when the ingredients are thoroughly amalgamated it\r
#Text=will be fit for use.
#Text=For a cold and cough, take a dessert-spoonful three\r
#Text=or four times a day, shaking or stirring it first.
#Text=RED LIP SALVE.
#Text=--Mix together equal portions of the best suet and the\r
#Text=best lard.
#Text=There must be no salt about them.
#Text=Boil slowly, and skim and\r
#Text=stir the mixture.
#Text=Then add a small thin bag of alkanet chips; and when\r
#Text=it has coloured the mixture of a fine deep red, take it out.
#Text=While\r
#Text=cooling, stir in, very hard, sufficient rose or orange-flower water to\r
#Text=give it a fine perfume.
#Text=A few drops of oil of rhodium will impart to it\r
#Text=a very agreeable rose-scent.
#Text=Cold cream for excoriated nostrils, chafed upper lips, or chapped hands\r
#Text=may be made nearly as above, but with one-third suet, and two-thirds\r
#Text=lard, and no alkanet.
#Text=When it has boiled thoroughly, remove it from the\r
#Text=fire, and stir in, gradually, a large portion of rose-water, or a little\r
#Text=oil of rhodium, beating very hard.
#Text=Put it into small gallicups, with\r
#Text=close covers.
#Text=ANTIDOTE TO CORROSIVE SUBLIMATE.
#Text=--If corrosive sublimate (one of the\r
#Text=worst poisons) has been swallowed, immediately drink a large quantity of\r
#Text=olive oil, even the whole contents of a flask; or more, indeed, if that\r
#Text=is not found sufficient.
#Text=This remedy, if taken in time, is always\r
#Text=efficacious.
#Text=If it cannot be immediately obtained, try white of egg.
#Text=REMEDY FOR AN OVER-DOSE OF LAUDANUM.
#Text=--A cup of the strongest possible\r
#Text=coffee has been known to keep the patient awake, and effect a positive\r
#Text=cure when all other means have failed.
#Text=After the fatal sleep has been\r
#Text=thus prevented, and the patient is thoroughly roused and excited, let an\r
#Text=emetic be administered.
#Text=BLACKBERRY SYRUP.
#Text=--Take a sufficient quantity of fine, ripe, sweet\r
#Text=blackberries.
#Text=Put them into a sieve placed over a large broad pan; and\r
#Text=with a clean potatoe-masher, (or something similar,) mash the\r
#Text=blackberries, and press out all their juice.
#Text=Or (having bruised them\r
#Text=first) put the blackberries into a linen bag, and squeeze out all the\r
#Text=juice into a vessel placed beneath.
#Text=Measure it; and to every quart of\r
#Text=the strained juice allow half a pound of powdered loaf-sugar; a heaped\r
#Text=tea-spoonful of powdered cinnamon; the same of powdered cloves; and a\r
#Text=large nutmeg grated.
#Text=Mix the spices with the juice and sugar, and boil\r
#Text=all together in a porcelain preserving-kettle; skimming it well.
#Text=When\r
#Text=cold, stir into each quart of the syrup half a pint of fourth-proof\r
#Text=brandy.
#Text=Then bottle it for use.
#Text=This is a good family medicine; and is\r
#Text=beneficial in complaints incident to summer.
#Text=It should be administered,\r
#Text=(at proper intervals,) from a tea-spoonful to a wine-glassful, according\r
#Text=to the age of the patient.
#Text=RHUBARB BITTERS.
#Text=--Take two ounces of rhubarb root; half an ounce of\r
#Text=cardamom seeds; one drachm of Virginia snake-root; and half a drachm of\r
#Text=gentian root.
#Text=Put these articles into a large bottle, and pour on it a\r
#Text=quart of good brandy.
#Text=It is excellent for children in complaints incident to summer weather.
#Text=LEMON HONEY.
#Text=--Take three large ripe lemons, (or four or five small\r
#Text=ones,) and (having rolled them under your hand on a table, to increase\r
#Text=the juice,) rub off on a piece of loaf-sugar the yellow rind or zest,\r
#Text=scraping it up with a teaspoon as you proceed, and putting it aside on a\r
#Text=saucer.
#Text=Then squeeze the juice of the lemons through a strainer, upon a\r
#Text=pound of loaf-sugar, (broken small or powdered,) and add the zest or\r
#Text=grated rind.
#Text=Cut up, among the sugar, a large quarter of a pound of the\r
#Text=best fresh butter.
#Text=Break six eggs into a shallow earthen pan, and beat\r
#Text=them till as light as possible.
#Text=Then mix in, gradually, the sugar and\r
#Text=lemon, stirring all very hard.
#Text=Put the whole into a porcelain-lined\r
#Text=kettle; set it over a moderate fire that has no blaze, and (stirring it\r
#Text=all the time) let it boil till it becomes of the consistence of very\r
#Text=thick honey.
#Text=If the weather is warm, you may add to its thickness by\r
#Text=stirring in a table-spoonful of ground arrow-root, or of sifted flour.
#Text=When done, put it warm into glass jars; cover them, closely, and seal\r
#Text=the covers.
#Text=It will keep in a cool dry place a month or more.
#Text=If made\r
#Text=in winter, it will continue good for two months.
#Text=RAISIN CURRANTS.
#Text=--Strip as many ripe currants from the stems as will\r
#Text=fill a quart measure when done.
#Text=Put them into a porcelain-lined kettle;\r
#Text=mash them, and add three quarters of a pound of sugar--brown will do.
#Text=Prepare three quarters of a pound of the largest and best raisins,\r
#Text=washed, drained, seeded, and cut in half.
#Text=Or, use the small sultana or\r
#Text=seedless raisins.
#Text=When the currants and sugar have come to a boil, and\r
#Text=been skimmed, mix in the raisins, gradually, and let them boil till\r
#Text=quite soft; skimming the surface well; and after each skimming stir the\r
#Text=whole down to the bottom of the kettle.
#Text=When done, take it up in a deep\r
#Text=dish, and set it to cool.
#Text=This is a nice, plain dessert.
#Text=For a larger quantity, take two quarts of stripped currants; a pound and\r
#Text=a half of sugar; and a pound and a half of raisins.
#Text=None but raisins of\r
#Text=the best quality should be used for this or any other purpose.
#Text=Low-priced raisins are unwholesome, being always of bad quality.
#Text=EXCELLENT PICKLED CABBAGE.
#Text=--Shred very fine, with a cabbage-cutter, a\r
#Text=large fresh red cabbage.
#Text=Pack it down (with a little salt sprinkled\r
#Text=between each layer) in a large stone jar.
#Text=The jar should be three parts\r
#Text=full of the shred cabbage.
#Text=Then tie up, in a bag of very thin clean\r
#Text=muslin, two table-spoonfuls of whole black pepper; the same quantity of\r
#Text=cloves; and the same of cinnamon, broken very small, but not powdered.
#Text=Also a dozen blades of mace.
#Text=Put two quarts of the best cider-vinegar\r
#Text=into a porcelain-lined kettle; throw in the bag of spices, and boil it.
#Text=Five minutes after it has come to a hard boil, take out the bag of\r
#Text=spice, and pour the vinegar hot over the cabbage in the jar; stirring it\r
#Text=up from the bottom, so that the vinegar may get all through the\r
#Text=cabbage.
#Text=Then lay the bag of spice on the top, and while the pickle is\r
#Text=hot, cover the jar closely.
#Text=It will be fit for use in two days.
#Text=If you find, after awhile, that the pickle tastes too much of the spice,\r
#Text=remove the spice-bag.
#Text=You may pickle white cabbage in the same way; omitting the cloves, and\r
#Text=boiling in the vinegar a second muslin bag, with three ounces of\r
#Text=turmeric, which will give the cabbage a fine bright yellow colour.
#Text=Having put up the cabbage into the jar, lay the turmeric-bag half way\r
#Text=down, and the spice-bag on the top.
#Text=But the turmeric-bag need not be put\r
#Text=into the jar if the vinegar has sufficiently coloured the cabbage.
#Text=Small onions may be pickled, as above, with turmeric.
#Text=Always, in\r
#Text=preparing onions, for any purpose, peel off the thin outer skin.
#Text=CHICKEN POT-PIE.
#Text=--Cut up, and par-boil a pair of large fowls, seasoning\r
#Text=them with pepper, salt, and nutmeg.
#Text=You may add some small slices of\r
#Text=cold ham; in which case add no salt, as the ham will make it salt\r
#Text=enough.
#Text=Or you may put in some pieces of the lean of fresh pork.
#Text=You may\r
#Text=prepare a suet-paste; but for a chicken pot-pie it is best to make the\r
#Text=paste of butter, which should be fresh, and of the best quality.
#Text=Allow\r
#Text=to each quart of flour, a small half-pound of butter.
#Text=There should be\r
#Text=enough for a great deal of paste.
#Text=Line the sides of the pot, two-thirds\r
#Text=up, with paste.
#Text=Put in the chickens, with the liquor in which they were\r
#Text=parboiled.
#Text=You may add some sliced potatoes.
#Text=Intersperse the pieces of\r
#Text=chicken with layers of paste in square slices.
#Text=Then cover the whole with\r
#Text=a lid of paste, not fitting very closely.
#Text=Make a slit in the top, and\r
#Text=boil the pie about three-quarters of an hour or more.
#Text=This pie will be greatly improved by adding some clams to the chickens\r
#Text=while par-boiling, omitting salt in the seasoning, as the clams will\r
#Text=salt it quite enough.
#Text=AN EASY WAY TO PICKLE MUSHROOMS.
#Text=--Take two quarts of small\r
#Text=freshly-gathered mushrooms.
#Text=With a sharp-pointed knife peel off,\r
#Text=carefully, their thin outside skin; and cut off the stalks closely.
#Text=Prepare eight little bags of very thin clear muslin, and tie up in each\r
#Text=bag six blades of mace; six slices of root-ginger; and a small nutmeg\r
#Text=(or half a large one) broken small, but not powdered.
#Text=Have ready four\r
#Text=glass jars, such as are considered to hold a quart.
#Text=Lay a bag of spice\r
#Text=in the bottom of each; then put in a pint of the mushrooms, laying a\r
#Text=second bag of spice on the top.
#Text=Have ready a sufficiency of the best\r
#Text=cider-vinegar, very slightly seasoned with salt; allowing to each quart\r
#Text=of vinegar but a salt-spoon of salt.
#Text=Fill up the jars with the vinegar,\r
#Text=finishing at the top with two table-spoonfuls of sweet oil.
#Text=Immediately\r
#Text=close up the jars, corking them tightly; and pasting thick paper, or\r
#Text=tying a piece of leather or bladder closely down over the corks.
#Text=These mushrooms will be found very fine; and as they require no cooking,\r
#Text=are speedily and easily prepared.
#Text=When a jar is once opened, it will be\r
#Text=well to use them fast.
#Text=They may be put up in small jars, or in glass\r
#Text=tumblers, such as hold but a pint altogether; seeing that the\r
#Text=proportions of spice in each jar or tumbler are duly divided, as above.
#Text=Keep them in a very dry place.
#Text=If you wish the mushrooms to be of a dark colour when pickled, add half\r
#Text=a dozen cloves to each bag of spice; but the clove-taste will most\r
#Text=likely overpower that of the mushrooms.
#Text=On no account omit the oil.
#Text=If you cannot obtain button-mushrooms, cut large ones into four\r
#Text=quarters, first peeling them and removing the stems.
